>> good afternoon. Um, I have changed my
stance on this a couple times and I want
to explain why I landed where I did. I
support tenant stability. I support
fairness, but I also support responsible
implementation and sound governance.
This proposal would apply to landlords
who own two properties or more. That is
a significant expansion of state law and
it carries real potential consequences
for housing supply, small property
owners, court operations, and
neighborhood stability. We must also be
honest about something else. Syracuse
already requires rental registration.
And here in Syracuse, we have not
consistently enforced that law. Before
expanding regulatory mandates, we should
demonstrate that we can effectively
enforce the laws already on the books.
Passing additional requirements without
enforcement capacity risks creating
policy on paper that we cannot implement
in practice. Before I was, excuse
[clears throat] me, before I was going
to cast this vote, I had three questions
that did not get answered. Does code
enforcement have the staffing, budget,
and operational plan to manage increased
inspections and complaints? Can housing
court absorb additional case load
without significant delays that hurt
both tenants and landlords? What
measurable impact will this expansion
have on small two-propy landlords who
make up a meaningful portion of our
housing stock? What is the concrete plan
to enforce existing rental registry and
housing code laws before layering on new
mandates? Because those answers have not
provided, I cannot support this proposal
today. That is why I vote no. Not
because I oppose tenant protections, but
because I believe major housing policy
decisions must be grounded in verified
capacity, data, and enforcability. It
would be irresponsible to move forward
today without that.
>> Thank you.
Thank you,

>> After a series of committee meetings,

Um before I read the resolution and um
that I'm asking us to adopt today, I
just want to if you indulge me for one
minute. Um, far too often the public
only sees the badge. Today I want to
talk about the heart behind it. I have a
few recent stories that our chief shared
with me that show what true community
policing looks like for our city. On
January 22nd, a resident called 911. She
found her husband unresponsive after
EMTs had done all they could. Two
officers arrived. One of them didn't
just stand there and file a report. He
walked past everyone in the home, went
straight to the grieving wife, and
simply held her in his her his arms
while she cried. A week later, officers
Roth and Henry stopped back at the home
to check on her. That's the level of
empathy that stays with a person
forever. On January 30, officer Tourett
Thorrett responded to a call for an
illegal eviction. She didn't just clear
the call, she stayed and called codes
and fire department to investigate.
Because she took the extra step, two
